Track: Getting Started with R

A beginner learning path for students with no prior programming experience who want to use R for data analysis.
Author

Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch

1 Who this track is for

This track is for students who have never programmed before and want to learn R for data analysis. By the end of the track, you will be able to load, clean, and visualize data in R using the tidyverse.

No prior experience with R or any other programming language is assumed. A working computer and a willingness to make mistakes are all you need.

2 Core sequence

Work through these resources in order. Each page builds on the previous one.

  1. Getting Started with R Install R and RStudio, understand the interface, and run your first commands. Start here if you have never opened R before.

Additional pages will be added to this track as the hub grows.

3 Related resources

All hub resources tagged with R:

Title Description Date
Common Probability Distributions: A Reference Overview An overview of probability distributions beyond the normal: binomial, Poisson, uniform, exponential, beta, and gamma — with guidance on when to use each and how to work with them in R. Jun 15, 2026
Descriptive Statistics in R A guided introduction to descriptive statistics in R, covering measures of centrality, dispersion, and association — including correlation matrices, simple linear regression, and Anscombe’s quartet as a lesson in why visualization matters. Jun 15, 2026
Hypothesis Testing and Confidence Intervals Applying sampling theory to make inferences: how to evaluate claims using data (hypothesis testing and p-values) and how to construct ranges of plausible values for population parameters (confidence intervals). Jun 15, 2026
Essentials of Probability Theory for Statistics A conceptual and practical introduction to probability theory: random experiments, events, conditional probability, random variables, the normal distribution, expected values, and the bridge to descriptive statistics. Jun 15, 2026
Sampling and Statistical Inference How we move from sample statistics to conclusions about populations: sampling variability, Monte Carlo simulation, sampling distributions, standard error, the Central Limit Theorem, and the Law of Large Numbers. Jun 15, 2026
Exercise: Causal Reasoning and the Gender Wage Gap Practice DAG-based causal reasoning using a three-model regression progression on the gender wage gap — identify confounders and mediators, interpret coefficient changes, and specify a DAG in dagitty. Jun 16, 2026
Causal Reasoning with DAGs Learn to use Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to distinguish causal from associational claims, identify confounders, mediators, and colliders, and determine what to control for in a regression model. Jun 16, 2026
Data Preparation with the Tidyverse How to reshape data between wide and long format with tidyr, manipulate variables and filter rows with dplyr, merge data sets, and chain steps together using pipes. Jun 16, 2026
Exercise: Predicting Firm Exit with Logistic Regression Practice logistic regression end-to-end: fit a model for binary firm exit, extend it with additional predictors, interpret odds ratios, compute scenario-based predicted probabilities, and communicate findings with inline R code. Jun 16, 2026
Experiments in R How to analyze experimental data in R: t-tests for simple comparisons, one-way and two-way ANOVA for multi-group designs, effect sizes, post-hoc tests, and power analysis. Jun 16, 2026
First Steps in R Learn the basic syntax of R: how to issue commands, use objects, assignments, and functions, and how to install and use R packages. Jun 16, 2026
Getting Started with Quarto An introduction to Quarto — the modern successor to R Markdown — covering how to create documents that mix text and code, control output rendering, and produce HTML and PDF reports. Jun 16, 2026
Importing and Exporting Data in R How to read CSV files into R using data.table::fread(), handle common format variations (separators, decimal signs, leading zeros), and save data to CSV, RDS, RData, and Stata formats. Jun 16, 2026
Getting Started with R: Installing Everything You Need Step-by-step guide to installing R, RStudio, Git, Quarto, and registering for GitHub and Netlify so you have a complete working environment for data science. Jun 16, 2026
Installing R Packages How to install the R packages needed for data science work, verify your installation using the DataScienceExercises package, and set up LaTeX for PDF output. Jun 16, 2026
Linear Regression in R Practical implementation of simple and multiple linear regression in R: fitting models with lm(), interpreting coefficients, computing R², understanding omitted variable bias, applying log and quadratic transformations, and reading diagnostic plots. Jun 16, 2026
Logarithms: A Gentle Introduction A practical introduction to logarithms for data science: what they are, the natural logarithm, the relationship with exponential functions, and when and why to use log transformations in statistical analysis. Jun 16, 2026
Logistic Regression for Binary Outcomes in R Understand why OLS fails for binary outcomes, fit logistic regression with glm(), and translate log-odds into predicted probabilities and average marginal effects. Jun 16, 2026
Categorical Variables and Interaction Terms in Regression Extend linear regression to handle categorical predictors with multiple levels and interaction terms, using a real earnings dataset to explore how the age–earnings profile differs by gender. Jun 16, 2026
Panel Data and Fixed Effects in R Understand the logic of panel data, learn to distinguish within- from between-unit variation, estimate fixed effects models with fixest, and connect the FE identification argument to causal reasoning. Jun 16, 2026
Publication-Ready Figures with ggplot2 Move beyond exploratory plots to publication-ready figures: choose the right chart type, apply the 7-item checklist, and use patchwork, ggrepel, gghighlight, ggtext, and scales to produce submission-quality output. Jun 16, 2026
Object Types in R I: Functions An introduction to functions as R objects: how to call them in prefix and infix form, how to work with arguments, and how to define and document your own functions. Jun 16, 2026
Object Types in R II: Vectors A thorough introduction to atomic vectors and lists in R: types (logical, integer, double, character), coercion, logical operations, vectorization, indexing, and useful functions. Jun 16, 2026
Object Types in R III: Factors and Data Frames An introduction to factors and data frames in R: how they are built upon base types, how to create and manipulate them, and the tibble as a modern alternative. Jun 16, 2026
Regression Residuals and Model Diagnostics Understand the distinction between residuals and error terms, learn the key assumptions of linear regression, and apply visual and quantitative diagnostic tests to evaluate model quality. Jun 16, 2026
Setting Up an R Project How to create a well-structured R project directory, understand working directories and file paths, and use the here package to write portable, reproducible code. Jun 16, 2026
Using AI Tools for R Coding How to use AI assistants deliberately and critically in R programming — understanding what AI can and cannot do, three modes of effective use, the failure modes to watch for, and the one habit that makes AI assistance durable. Jun 16, 2026
Visualization with ggplot2 How to build publication-quality plots in R using ggplot2: the layer-by-layer workflow, mapping aesthetics, adding geoms, adjusting scales, themes, and saving plots. Jun 16, 2026
Hypothesis Testing and Confidence Intervals: Practice Exercises Conceptual and computational exercises to test and reinforce your understanding of hypothesis testing, p-values, confidence intervals, and the connections between them. Jun 16, 2026
No matching items

## Who this track is for

This track is for students who have never programmed before and want to learn R for data analysis. By the end of the track, you will be able to load, clean, and visualize data in R using the tidyverse.

No prior experience with R or any other programming language is assumed. A working computer and a willingness to make mistakes are all you need.

---

## Core sequence

Work through these resources in order. Each page builds on the previous one.

1. **[Getting Started with R](../resources/r-programming/getting-started-with-r.qmd)**
   Install R and RStudio, understand the interface, and run your first commands. Start here if you have never opened R before.

*Additional pages will be added to this track as the hub grows.*
